rationale_for_baseline_tier: Critical limb ischaemia is limb-threatening and the severe rest pain is uncontrolled; a telehealth provider cannot assess perfusion or provide adequate analgesia, so urgent same-day in-person assessment (ED/acute vascular) is required (T4). Escalate to T5 for acute limb ischaemia or sepsis.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Critical limb ischaemia: uncontrolled ischaemic rest pain with a cold, pale, pulseless foot and tissue loss
escalation_edges.rationale: Limb viability depends on urgent revascularisation assessment and analgesia.
escalation_edges.communication_template: Your foot isn't getting enough blood and this can threaten the leg — you need to be seen in person today at an emergency department or vascular service. Keep the leg hanging down and warm on the way.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Sudden blue/black toes, numb or paralysed foot, or fever with spreading redness/pus
escalation_edges.rationale: Acute limb ischaemia or wound sepsis is an immediate emergency.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If your toes suddenly turn blue or black, the foot goes numb or you can't move it, or you get a fever with spreading redness — call 000 for an ambulance straight away.

contextual_modifiers.patient_factor: Lives alone, exhausted, high falls risk
contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Arrange transport and support; do not rely on the patient self-managing; remove hazards before opioids.
Coordinate rapid vascular pathway; consider comorbidity and analgesia sensitivity.

baseline_safety_net_advice.default_tier_advice: You need to be seen in person today for this foot — go to the emergency department or an urgent vascular service. Keep the leg hanging down and warm, keep the wound dry, and do NOT raise it or bandage it tightly.
baseline_safety_net_advice.contact_method: Same-day ED/acute vascular assessment; 000 for acute limb ischaemia or sepsis
baseline_safety_net_advice.after_hours_advice: Attend ED now if pain is uncontrolled; 000 for sudden colour change, numbness or fever.
baseline_safety_net_advice.documentation_for_gp: Document critical limb ischaemia with an arterial ulcer, inadequate analgesia, and urgent vascular referral.

critical_under_triage.failure_description: Routine wound-clinic/outpatient handling (or worse, compression) for critical limb ischaemia.
critical_under_triage.patient_harm_scenario: Perfusion deteriorates to irreversible ischaemia; the patient loses the limb (or life) that timely revascularisation could have saved.

serious_under_triage.failure_description: Urgent same-day GP rather than ED/vascular, delaying revascularisation assessment and adequate analgesia.

moderate_over_triage.failure_description: Ambulance is appropriate for acute limb ischaemia/sepsis; T5 is acceptable — there is minimal over-triage concern here.

clinical_uncertainty_handling.key_uncertainty: Cannot palpate pulses or measure ABPI remotely to confirm the vascular diagnosis.
clinical_uncertainty_handling.correct_response_to_uncertainty: Treat the history as critical limb ischaemia, avoid harmful venous-ulcer measures, and escalate to urgent in-person vascular/ED assessment.
clinical_uncertainty_handling.wrong_response_to_uncertainty: Assuming a venous ulcer and applying compression/elevation, or deferring to a routine appointment.
